:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 2,743 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 10% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

Each crime in Louisiana appears to have a statutory maximum. For example, theft of something with a value of $25,000 or over will result in imprisonment of not more than twenty years or a fine of not more than fifty thousand dollars, or both. Any sentence to imprisonment must be “determinate,” in other words, judges sentence individuals to a maximum sentence rather than a range with minimum and maximum limits. Eligibility for parole is determined by type of crime and by criminal history. Unusually, prisoners who have been convicted of non-violent, non-sexual offenses can also age out of the Louisiana prison system even if they are ineligible for parole. If sentenced to a term of over 30 years, a prisoner who has served at least 20 of those years and has reached the age of 45 is eligible for parole consideration. If sentenced to any term without the benefit of parole, a prisoner who has reached the age of 60 and has completed various institutional requirements may be eligible for parole.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Hispanic, any race people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison for a new crime, accounting for 100% (6 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 1,803 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 66% of the parole-eligible prison population in prison for new crimes.

``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
